Patch Set 9: Code-Review-1

(1 comment)

This seems a lot more complex than the original solution of just passing the PATH to the remote side.  I think the best way to do this is to use the patch #8 implementation, but rather than hard-coding the PATH, use getnev("PATH") and send that to the remote side with rsh.

Patch Set 9: Verified+1

Tests uploaded by Onyx Autotest from onyx-31vm1 for review-dne-part-2 received by maloo, run on CentOS Linux 7/x86_64: (https://testing.hpdd.intel.com/test_sessions/2b8fad06-0a83-4c5a-bbed-d02187a82993). Ran 16 tests. No failures.

Patch Set 9:

(1 comment)

>This seems a lot more complex than the original solution of just passing the PATH to the remote side.  I think the best way to do this is to use the patch #8 implementation, but rather than hard-coding the PATH, use getnev("PATH") and send that to the remote side with rsh.

The getnev("PATH") only tells us what the executable paths are, but it may be not all, it does not means the remote shell can find related zpool/zfs tools on remote side, just as Oleg pointed out in the set8' comment. That is why I made the set 9.

The set 9 does not hard coded the PATH environment variable, it allows the admin to specify the  remote PATH case by case, that is more flexible than set 8 implementation.

As for the complexity, I am not sure what you mean. The set 9 implementation abstracts the format-string with micro definition. I think it is helpful for readers to understand what the command will do, and also helps the developers to avoid typo trouble.

(1 comment)

I think we should be more aware of shell expansion problems when we do system() sort of commands.

This is both for the PATH setting and some of the otter things like when there are spaces or shell expansion symbols in the name.

Patch Set 11:

(1 comment)

Patch Set 11:

Oleg, as explained in former comment, the lsnapshot is sysadmin tool and run with root privilege. We trust that the sysadmin is harmless, otherwise he/she can damage the system via lctl arbitrarily, no need hack lsnapshot.

As for the PATH variable, non-privilege user cannot change root user's PATH variable. So even if some malicious user wanted to hack root privilege via bad PATH variable, he/she cannot affect the root's environment.

parents.subject: LU-9764 lfsck: reset LFSCK trace file if fail to load it

commit.subject: LU-9751 snapshot: set PATH for remote zfs commands
commit.message:
LU-9751 snapshot: set PATH for remote zfs commands

It is possible that the remote zfs/zpool commands for Lustre
snapshot are NOT in the remote shell execute/search path. So
needs to set the PATH variable for the remote shell commands.

It is inconvenient for the admin to specify the PATH option
via single lsnapshot command for each Lustre target. So the
patch specifies the remote PATH environment variable as the
the local PATH environment variable. It requires all Lustre
servers to have broadly consistent zfs tools instalation in
such PATH.

It also contains some macro definations for code cleanup.

Signed-off-by: Fan Yong <fan.yong@intel.com>
Change-Id: I2b1ce630d4aad63ab20e6c323f2222dccb51ed6e
Reviewed-on: https://review.whamcloud.com/27999
